data.title: What The Market Got Wrong With Uber (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Uber Earnings rundown

Uber Technologies, Inc. (UBER) is back on my radar after the recent pullback. The stock has been in limbo for the past few months, down over 15% year-to-date and touching a recent low of $68.40 on Friday, and is now flat on Monday. Uber went out of the market’s favor after Q4 earnings, which disappointed investors, with revenue of $14.37 billion, above the $14.32 billion consensus, up from $12 billion in the year-ago quarter. The closely watched mobility segment was up 19% year over year to $8.2 billion, slightly missing consensus at $8.3 billion, while delivery revenue was up 30% during the same period to $4.9 billion, above consensus at $4.72 billion.

Delivery was something I flagged when I last covered the stock in September of last year, and it seems to be gaining momentum.

Uber's Q4 net income of $296 million looked weak against the prior year's $6.88 billion, but the comparison doesn’t give the full picture, as the drop was driven by a $1.6 billion pre-tax headwind from equity investment revaluations, higher taxes, and deliberate pricing investments to drive affordability and user acquisition. Strip that out, and the operating picture was clean, with gross bookings of $54.1 billion beating the $53.1 billion consensus; monthly active consumers hit 202 million, up 18% year-over-year, and management expects at least 17% gross bookings growth to $52 billion to $53.5 billion in Q1 '26. The softness in reported profits was the cost of buying growth, and by the bookings and user metrics, it's working.

Trips per vehicle per day on Uber's platform are running 30% higher than standalone first-party AV platforms, with better ETAs. CEO Dara Khosrowshahi also made the structural point that Uber's delivery and freight network allows it to utilize AV vehicles during mobility demand troughs, which is a cross-network utilization advantage that no pure-play AV operator can replicate.

“Uber accelerated into another record-breaking quarter, with more than 200 million monthly users completing more than 40 million trips every day—our largest and most engaged consumer base ever…We enter 2026 with a rapidly growing topline, significant cash flow, and a clear path to becoming the largest facilitator of AV trips in the world,” said Khosrowshahi in the earnings report.

Stock performance and valuation

Along with market uncertainty around the war in Iran and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, the stock is suffering, and mostly moving in sympathy with the broader market. Still, let's look at the glass half full; Uber is the kind of company that gets thrown out with the bathwater in a macro selloff, which is exactly what's happened here. The business hasn't deteriorated, and the market has simply re-rated everything with exposure to consumer discretionary spending and global growth. At current levels, that creates an entry point worth paying attention to.

Uber is down around 7% over the past year, down another 30% over the past 6 months, down 8% over the past month, and down another 8% over the past 5 days alone. Year to date, the story doesn’t look that different for Uber, but it definitely looks worse for Lyft (LYFT) and DoorDash (DASH), as seen below, with both Lyft and Dash down over 30% each, double that of Uber’s pullback.

Valuation has also just gotten way more attractive than it was a month ago, though the business continues to grow. Uber trades at 12.86x forward EV/EBITDA, less than half of DoorDash's 16.98x, and on a business with a 27.39% forward EBITDA growth rate. The most striking single data point is the forward PEG of 0.91, below 1.0, implying the market is not giving Uber full credit for its growth rate, which is unusual for a platform of this scale and quality.

Layer in a levered free cash flow 3-year CAGR of 125.85%, an EBITDA margin of 12.13% against Lyft's negative 0.53%, and a 10.70% EBIT margin that neither competitor comes close to matching, and the profitability profile starts to look like a business that has quietly crossed a maturity threshold the market hasn't fully repriced.

At 2.43x forward EV/Sales and 13.95x price-to-cash flow, investors are paying a reasonable multiple at a point in the cycle where sentiment has temporarily disconnected from fundamentals. The stock is trading at around $69 and has been more or less trading sideways since mid-February, when it also traded at $69. While the stock broke the EMA21 resistance level at $73 last week, it didn’t hold. The stock is also trading below the EMA50 at $76 and the EMA200 at $82. RSI is also nearing oversold territory, though it's not there yet, at 36. With all EMAs trading downwards, the stock remains above that $86.4 low. As long as it holds above that level and gains momentum over the next trading days, I think the risk-reward is favorable here.

Investment thesis

I think the opportunity here outweighs the risks, and the AV story is the real catalyst. Uber now has 20-plus AV partners globally and expects to be operating in 15 cities by year-end, with a deal to invest up to $1.25 billion in Rivian (RIVN) to deploy 50,000 robotaxis already in motion. The strategic logic is airtight since Uber doesn't need to build the cars. Uber works as an operating layer that every AV manufacturer routes through, and it already does.

The company is also now working hand in hand with Nvidia (NVDA), and that is about one of the best partnerships you can have in the AI and autonomy era. Nvidia's DRIVE platform is the compute backbone that most AV developers are building on, and Uber sits at the other end of that stack as the distribution and commercialization layer. According to the press release, back in mid-March, both companies,

“announced an expansion of their autonomous vehicle partnership. The companies, supported by their growing roster of automaker partners, plan to launch a global fleet of entirely NVIDIA software-driven autonomous vehicles, starting in Los Angeles and San Francisco in the first half of 2027 and scaling across 28 cities globally by 2028.”

According to Nvidia’s CEO Jensen Huang,

“The ‘ChatGPT moment’ for physical AI has arrived—robotic systems can now reason about the complexities of the physical world…Uber is building one of the world’s most expansive autonomous ride-hailing platforms. We are delighted to connect NVIDIA’s large ecosystem of robotaxi-ready partners to the Uber network to bring the magic of robotaxis to cities worldwide.”

The broader AV partnership roster reinforces the network effect dynamic that makes Uber's position defensible. With 20-plus AV partners, spanning Google (GOOG) backed Waymo, Motional, Cruise, Avride, Rivian, and now Nvidia, Uber has avoided the trap of betting on a single technology winner. The platform grows stronger as the ecosystem expands, the opposite of how most hardware-dependent businesses scale.

I’m upgrading the stock to a strong buy at current levels.

Risks

With every investment, there’s risk, and the case with Uber is no different, which is why the bear case deserves a fair hearing, particularly in this macro environment. The most immediate risk for Uber, in my opinion, is consumer spending sensitivity. Uber is a discretionary service at its core, and with U.S. inflation running closer to 3%, and gasoline prices surging on the back of the Hormuz closure, the consumer backdrop is deteriorating in ways that directly pressure trip frequency and average order values.

Still, Uber doesn't own a single car and doesn't pay for a drop of fuel, as the entire cost of vehicle ownership, maintenance, insurance, and gasoline falls on the independent driver. That asset-light structure is precisely what insulates Uber's income statement from the energy shock that is currently devastating traditional transportation businesses. The risk is there, even if it comes indirectly. If gasoline prices rise enough, driver supply could tighten as the economics of driving for Uber deteriorate, pushing up incentive costs to attract and retain drivers.

The stock is now down over 29% since my Buy back in September.

While I wouldn’t be surprised with some near-term volatility with the overall market on edge and no end to the war in sight, I think the risk-reward for Uber is favorable.

data.title: Corcept Gains FDA Approval In Ovarian Cancer (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Just over one month ago, I upgraded Corcept Therapeutics Incorporated (CORT) to Buy following the company's loss in its patent litigation against Teva Pharmaceuticals (TEVA). This was a setback for the company, allowing Teva to release a generic version of Corcept's drug Korlym and potentially calling into question the legacy Cushing's syndrome franchise that delivered $761M in 2025 sales.

Counterintuitively, this development marked a bottom for the stock, pricing in the bad news, while upside from Corcept's new drug Lifyorli (relacorilant) provided a catalyst to move higher. Indeed, the stock had been punished severely in 2025 due to the patent litigation loss and the FDA deciding not to move forward with an approval of relacorilant for Cushing's syndrome. The latter would have enabled Corcept to protect its Korlym sales by switching patients to the newer drug.

However, the stock market is forward-looking, and uncertainty has now started to clear. Analyzing Corcept's balance sheet, I became bullish because the company had ample liquidity to pivot to ovarian cancer, reinventing itself into a growing oncology franchise. On Wednesday, the FDA issued a decision that came earlier than anticipated, granting approval for relacorilant in platinum-resistant ovarian cancer (the PDUFA date had been mid-July).

This development is bullish for Corcept because the regulatory risk surrounding approval of relacorilant is gone. Now the question is execution: can the company grow sales of its newly approved drug quickly enough to offset generic competition of Korlym? Due to the quality of data, the breadth of the approval, and the first-in-class mechanism of a selective glucocorticoid receptor antagonist (SGRA) for oncology, I believe that the odds are now in Corcept's favor. While the stock has rallied from $33 to $38 since my last article, there is ample upside ahead if Corcept can successfully commercialize its new drug. As such, I believe that the odds have improved for shareholders of Corcept and accordingly raise my rating to Strong Buy.

Relacorilant: ROSELLA Data and FDA Approval

Earlier this year, the FDA assigned Lifyorli (relacorilant) a Prescription Drug User Fee Act (PDUFA) target date of July 11, 2026. However, in a massive win for Corcept, the FDA issued its approval early on March 25, 2026.

To recap the pivotal Phase 3 ROSELLA data that drove this expedited approval: the trial enrolled 381 patients and met its dual primary endpoints of progression-free survival (PFS) and overall survival (OS). The combination of Lifyorli plus nab-paclitaxel reduced the risk of disease progression by 30% compared to nab-paclitaxel alone (HR: 0.70; p=0.008).

Overall survival data also proved to be robust. Median OS was prolonged by 4.1 months, with the Lifyorli arm achieving 16.0 months compared to 11.9 months for chemotherapy alone (HR: 0.65; p=0.0004). That translates to a 35% reduction in the risk of death in a notoriously difficult-to-treat patient population. The drug is biomarker-agnostic and was reported to be well tolerated, avoiding the severe compounding toxicities often seen in oncology combinations.

According to Alexander Olawaiye, M.D., at Magee-Womens Hospital of the University of Pittsburgh, these data support "this regimen becoming the standard-of-care treatment" in platinum-resistant ovarian cancer. This sentiment was echoed in the FDA approval press release by Dr. Rob Coleman of Texas Oncology, who emphasized that having a new treatment with this survival benefit will provide clinicians with a highly compelling new option.

Corcept Therapeutics: Analyst Projections for Sales Growth

With Lifyorli cleared for the market, the investment thesis for Corcept has transitioned to a bet on the company's commercial execution. For 2026, Corcept issued guidance of $900M to $1B in sales.

In my previous article, I modeled the potential sales of Lifyorli against declining sales of Korlym, which now faces generic competition. However, because Korlym is sold to treat a rare disease, its sales should fall more slowly than the majority of small molecules facing a patent cliff.

The primary unknown is now Lifyorli's total addressable market. Corcept has set a wholesale list price of $37,900 for a 28-day cycle. Approximately 20,000 women in the U.S. alone are candidates to start a new therapy for platinum-resistant ovarian cancer each year. While conservative estimates from analysts like UBS project peak sales for Lifyorli in this specific indication at around $550 million, other analysts see a clear path to blockbuster status. Some long-term consensus forecasts suggest that as relacorilant expands its label into other chemotherapy-resistant malignancies (like prostate and pancreatic cancer), positioning the drug to achieve global peak sales north of $2 billion by the early 2030s.

The analyst consensus sees growth from Lifyorli as sufficient to offset losses from Korlym. If this is the case, the stock is reasonably cheap. In 2026, Corcept should retain profitability, but as the commercialization unfolds in 2027, sales of $1.19B would put the stock around 23x forward earnings.

Following the approval, Wall Street analysts have scrambled to revise their models, reflecting the de-risking of Corcept’s pipeline. Upgrades have been swift, with firms like Canaccord Genuity boosting their price targets to $110, citing the sheer commercial magnitude of the ovarian cancer catalyst.

Although it is difficult to precisely call a turnaround, all the ingredients are in place, which should allow the market's focus to shift away from the generic threats facing Korlym, toward the massive potential of Corcept's newly validated oncology franchise.

Conclusion

The narrative surrounding Corcept has reversed more quickly than I had envisioned due to the rapid FDA approval. Recent patent litigation losses and disappointing news from the clinic last year created a cloud of pessimism that drove shares to a heavy discount, pricing in the worst-case scenario for the company. However, investors who were willing to look past that to the promise of the oncology franchise now have gains even though the market has fallen sharply due to geopolitical risks.

Backed by a pristine balance sheet, excellent clinical data, and a highly anticipated commercial launch, the risk/reward profile is now skewed to the upside. Corcept has moved beyond defending its past and is now defining its future. Accordingly, I upgrade my rating on CORT to a Strong Buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Neumora Therapeutics: Downgrading After Navacaprant Development Setback
data.description:
The last time I spoke about Neumora Therapeutics (NMRA), it was with a Seeking Alpha article entitled "Neumora: Kostal-1 Readout For MDD Treatment Is First Study On Deck." With respect to this article, I mentioned that it would release data from its KOASTAL-1 study using its drug navacaprant for the treatment of patients with major depressive disorder [MDD] by the end of 2024. It finally released results from this, the first of three replicate studies from its KOASTAL program. Today, I'm downgrading this stock to a "Hold" rating from a "Strong Buy" rating. The first reason is because of the fact that it failed to achieve the primary endpoint of reduction in depressive symptoms as measured by MADRS total score compared to placebo. The company did change the protocol for the other studies as part of the KOASTAL program, KOASTAL-2 and KOASTAL-3, which is a good thing.

It increased enrollment for each of these studies and expects a topline data readout from each in Q2 of 2026. While this approach might end up working out, the fact is that the company has a steep uphill battle to climb here, in my opinion. It's still a risk, as increasing the number of patients is not guaranteed to generate a study win. The second reason why I'm downgrading to keep this stock at a "Hold" rating is because the company did achieve a highly successful outcome using another drug from its pipeline, known as V1aR antagonist NMRA-511, to treat patients with Alzheimer's Disease [AD] agitation. In the modified analysis set [MAS], with a total of 71 patients given this drug with twice-daily [BID] dosing, achieved a placebo-adjusted change from baseline on the CMAI total score of -2.6 and -2.1 was achieved at weeks 6 and 8, respectively.

A further enhanced effect of CMAI score in the same time period in patients with elevated anxiety was achieved. The thing is that this was a signal-finding study, and this study was not powered to detect statistical significance. Where potential shareholder value could come from would be in the 2nd half of 2026. That is when it expects to release additional multiple ascending dose [MAD] data from this phase 1b study using NMRA-511 to treat these AD agitation patients. The downside is that a phase 2 study for this very same program is not expected to be initiated until Q1 of 2027.

Phase 1b Signal-Seeking Study Shows Promise For Treating Alzheimer's Disease Agitation

As I noted above, Neumora Therapeutics had failed to achieve statistical significance from its phase 3 KOASTAL-1 study using its drug navacaprant for the treatment of patients with major depressive disorder [MDD]. The hope is that by modifying the other studies [KOASTAL-2 and KOASTAL-3] to exceed initial target enrollment, it can redeem this drug and this particular program. With more than 400 patients enrolled in each of these studies, it believes this will be enough to show a drug effect. This remains to be seen, and I believe it is a major risk since the first phase 3 study failed to achieve statistical significance with respect to the MADRS total score. A joint topline readout from both of these studies is expected in Q2 of 2026.

However, for the purpose of this article, I want to go over a program that I believe it can potentially do well in the long run with. This would be in terms of advancing its V1aR antagonist NMRA-511 for the treatment of patients with Alzheimer's Disease [AD] agitation in the phase 1b study. Before going over this clinical program in detail, plus any catalysts to come about from it, I believe that it is first important to go over what this disorder is and what the possible market opportunity for the company could end up being. First and foremost, it is important to understand what Alzheimer's Disease [AD] is. This disorder is characterized as a brain disorder that tears away at a person's memory and thinking skills. This disorder is not a normal part of aging at all, and it is believed that there are two reasons that cause it, and they are:

  • Buildup of amyloid plaques
  • Neurofibrillary tau tangles in the brain

A result of this is brain cell death and shrinking of the brain, leading to multiple problems, like inability to think properly, remember things, or lack of judgment for decision-making. About 60% to 80% of cases of dementia are caused by Alzheimer's Disease. With that being said, agitation comes as a separate diagnosis that a patient would have. Alzheimer's Disease agitation stems from fear, change in surroundings, change in caregiver, and many other items. There are many symptoms that these AD agitation patients experience, but they are broken down into three categories:

  • Verbal abuse
  • Physical aggression
  • Excessive physical activity

In terms of the last bullet point directly above, "Excessive physical activity," this can be a patient wandering around a lot, pacing, or being restless. What about the fact that this company is only going after agitation for these AD patients? Even if going after a portion of AD patients, it still ends up being a large enough market opportunity for it. First and foremost, as more people age, the population of AD patients in the United States is expected to grow. Secondly, about >70% of these patients experience agitation at some point in their disease [Neumora Therapeutics Corporate Presentation March 2026, slide #8]. It still remains a huge unmet medical need for sure. The first FDA-approved drug to treat AD agitation is REXULTI [brexpiprazole]. As I will show below, there is a chance for this drug to be competitive in this space against both REXULTI and maybe even AUVELITY [dextromethorphan hydrobromide and bupropion hydrochloride]. There was not only a substantial treatment effect in the phase 1b study using NMRA-511 to treat these AD agitation patients, but the company also did further work comparing two certain populations within this one. One subpopulation was the evaluation of the drug in patients with elevated anxiety levels [with a higher treatment effect observed], and then the second one was the targeting of patients with a certain cutoff level of Neuropsychiatric Inventory Agitation/Aggression [NPI-AA] score ≥4. This last one is important because this is what was used as enrichment of recruited patients for late-stage studies.

In order to determine if NMRA-511 would be good enough to be able to treat Alzheimer's Disease agitation patients, the company is in the process of running a phase 1b study. This early-stage study was set up in a way to establish two different parts as follows:

  • Part A: Healthy elderly patients for safety, tolerability, and pharmacokinetic [PK] profile
  • Part B: Randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled portion of study that recruited 80 AD agitation patients given 80 mg of NMRA-511 BID [twice daily]

As I mentioned above, the modified analysis set [MAS] included a total number of 71 patients. There was a -2.6 and -2.1 placebo-adjusted change from baseline on CMAI at weeks 6 and 8, respectively. The whole point is to see if the drug can have a greater effect on reducing symptoms of agitation for these AD patients. CMAI stands for "Cohen-Mansfield Agitation Inventory" and it is a 29-item scoring scale, with each item being scored from a range of "1" to never being observed, to "7" agitation showing up several times per hour. The highest score that can be attained for this is 203 points. The Cohen's d effect size range for this MAS patient population was pretty good at 0.20 – 0.23. However, the Cohen's d effect size range for the other two subpopulations was higher. These effect sizes are shown as follows and prove that the company can enrich its study further in phase 2 to only recruit AD agitation patients with elevated levels of anxiety [Neumora Therapeutics Corporate Presentation March 2026, slide #16]:

  • Elevated anxiety patients - Cohen's d effect size of 0.51 – 0.64
  • Neuropsychiatric Inventory Agitation/Aggression (NPI-AA) score ≥4 [Other sponsors used this pool of patients for late-stage studies - Cohen's d effect size of 0.32 – 0.34

The NPI-AA score between NMRA-511 and REXULTI is pretty comparable. Where the company could create an advantage is the effect of targeting these patients with elevated levels of anxiety.

The beauty of the data above is that this company is not limited in its dosing of NMRA-511. The reason why is that it saw no safety signals with the doses used to date, and there is a plan underway to evaluate higher doses in the MAD portion of this phase 1b study. Data from the MAD cohort expansion data from this early-stage study using this drug to treat these patients is expected in the 2nd half of 2026. Not only that, but there is the expectation that it will be able to initiate a phase 2 study using NMRA-511 to treat these AD agitation patients in Q1 of 2027.

Financials

According to the 10-K SEC Filing, Neumora Therapeutics had cash and cash equivalents of $182.5 million as of December 31, 2025. The company completed its IPO back in September of 2023 and raised approximately $226.5 million after deducting expenses. Since then, it has deployed a few financial tools to raise additional cash. In October of 2024, it enacted an at-the-market equity offering program with Leerink, where it could sell up to an aggregate of $300 million in total gross proceeds from time to time as needed. For the years ending December 31, 2025, and 2024, it raised approximately $19.7 million and $13.7 million, respectively.

In May of 2025, it entered into a loan security agreement with K2 Health Ventures LLC for an aggregate principal amount of $125 million. However, it is important to state that it only borrowed $60 million from this. The other $65 million for borrowing will be at the company's request, along with the fact that it has to meet certain clinical milestones as well in order to obtain it. The company has plenty of cash on hand to fund its operations for an extended period of time. Why do I state this? That's because it believes it has enough cash on hand to fund its operations into Q3 of 2027. The company burns about $58.5 million of cash per quarter. This is broken down into $44.7 million in R&D expenses and then $13.8 million in G&A expenses.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Neumora Therapeutics. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the ongoing evaluation of navacaprant for the treatment of patients with MDD. The company had not met the primary endpoint of change from baseline in MADRS total score at Week 6. The company decided to increase enrollment to see if it can do better with its phase 3 KOASTAL-2 and KOASTAL-3 studies. The risk here is that even with this increase in enrollment to improve the effect of this drug, there is no assurance that this will happen. If both trials fail to achieve statistical significance, even after the changes made to them, then there is a chance that this program will be cut from its pipeline.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the development of V1aR antagonist NMRA-511 for the treatment of patients with AD agitation in the ongoing phase 1b study. The risk here is that upon releasing the MAD data, utilizing higher doses of the drug, there is no assurance that the efficacy observed thus far will be improved upon. Another risk is in terms of what was compared to Cohen's d effect size between NMRA-511 and REXULTI/AUVELITY. This data is not concrete, and making cross-trial comparisons should always be taken with a grain of salt. The final risk is that of the increased effect size in terms of efficacy observed from the phase 1b study using this drug to target AD agitation patients with elevated levels of anxiety. Even though there was a -7.6 and -5.6 placebo-adjusted change from baseline on the CMAI total score at both Weeks 6 and 8, there is no guarantee that this data will be replicated in additional clinical studies. The hope is that by enriching future studies with these patients, the company has a better shot at a successful outcome, but this remains to be seen.

The third and final risk to consider would be the selection of the M4 positive allosteric modulator [PAM] NMRA-898 for the treatment of patients with schizophrenia. This was selected based on positive findings from a phase 1 study, plus the half-life of 80 – 100 hours could allow for a competitive once-daily dosing treatment. This program still has a while to go, because the next step is to initiate a MAD portion of this phase 1 study, which is going to target both healthy volunteers and stable patients with schizophrenia. There is a need to establish a maximum tolerated dose [MTD] and confirm Central Nervous System [CNS] penetration via CSF exposure. The risk here is that there is no guarantee that the higher doses tested won't cause any major side effects. Nor that the drug is going to be able to penetrate the CNS as intended in a proper manner. With data coming from this MAD portion of this trial in the 2nd half of 2026, there is no guarantee that it will be reported in a positive manner. Nor that the data produced from it will be enough to move forward into the next stage of clinical testing.

Conclusion

Neumora Therapeutics had a major problem when it was not able to achieve the primary endpoint of change from baseline in MADRS total score from its KOASTAL-1 study in a statistically significant manner. It is believed that the protocol changes it made to its other ongoing phase 3 KOSTAL-2 and KOASTAL-3 studies are going to give it a better shot at meeting the intended primary endpoint, but this remains to be seen. This was the primary reason for my downgrading of this stock from a "Strong Buy" rating to a "Hold." Furthermore, I think it should be a "Hold" because of the positive signals it achieved from its phase 1b signal-seeking study using NMRA-511 to treat patients with AD agitation.

The latest Q4 and full-year 2025 update for earnings noted that this drug had an unsurpassed clinical effect in a pre-specified population comparable to the studies done with that of REXULTI and AUVELITY. This is the NPI-AA score I went over above. With the NMRA-511 program still on track to release higher multi-dose data from the phase 1b study for the targeting of patients with AD agitation, plus the protocol change for navacaprant to treat patients with MDD, I believe that this stock should be downgraded to a "Hold" rating. Should things improve during 2026, then I will have to reevaluate the stock at such time.

data.title: Too Early To Call The Bottom
data.description:
I see a similar pattern to what happened nearly a year ago during Trump's tariff sell-off.

However, I am not buying the dip yet despite the fundamentals of the high-growth pockets in the market (mainly beneficiaries from the AI data center buildout) remaining intact.

Trying to time the bottom of a selloff is often a sucker's bet, but I'm more than happy to take this one. Why?

So far, I am not seeing any signs of capitulation. Since the war began, the SPY hasn't seen a single 2% drop in one day. Let alone a 4% drop like in April last year. On top of that, the S&P 500 is still in a very awkward place right now from a technical perspective, with poor breadth in pretty much all sectors except energy.

In this piece, I will focus on the most recent data that tries to put a number on the global impact of higher oil prices due to the conflicts in the Middle East.

My view is that this data is overly pessimistic. The U.S. consumer is starting to sweat about higher-for-longer gas prices, although I take the survey data with skepticism.

Overall, I am staying on the sidelines, as I don't think the lows are behind us. I still see the SPY at $610-$620 in the first half of the year, followed by a rally in the second half, as the oil shock fades (similarly to the tariff fears last year).

This is an exogenous event, and I strongly believe it will be transitory, even though "experts" suggest otherwise. I discuss more below.

OECD Cuts the Quality of Its Global Outlook

The Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development released its interim report last Thursday, showing a 1.2 percentage point increase in inflation to 4.0% for G20 countries this year.

The reason I'm highlighting this report is that it's the first main study since the war that tries to quantify the impact of higher for longer oil prices due to the Strait of Hormuz being blocked.

Notably, this organization expects a headline inflation of 4.2% in the US this year. For reference, the February CPI showed only 2.4% yoy, and the Cleveland Fed Inflation Nowcast estimate for March is showing 3.16%.

Looking at GDP, the OECD sees U.S. growth slowing from 2.0% in 2026 to 1.7% in 2027.

By the way, the OECD is assuming the following:

These projections assume that the current energy market disruption is temporary, with prices easing from mid 2026 onward.

Given the recent headlines suggesting the possibility of a U.S. invasion of Kharg Island, if this were to happen, I think the OECD's assumption of oil prices easing from mid-2026 may be too optimistic.

Increasing Stress In The Bond Markets

Here is a "fun fact" (well, it's fun only if you are sitting on cash).

The 3-month MOVE index (which measures the implied volatility of the Treasury market) experienced its biggest monthly rise since the global financial crisis.

Over the last four weeks, roughly $75 billion of Treasuries have been liquidated, and the bid-ask spreads in short-dated Treasuries widened close to 30%.

Looking at the latest Treasury auctions, there was an extremely weak two-year sale on March 24 and another weak seven-year auction on March 26.

Now, despite last week's poor auction data, I am not panicking (yet) about liquidity and foreign demand for U.S. Treasuries. The latest data (January 2026) shows $9.305T in total foreign holdings, representing an increase of about 8% yoy.

A Deteriorating Consumer Sentiment in the U.S.

Given what’s going on in the Middle East, I wasn’t surprised to see the University of Michigan report that its March consumer sentiment index fell to 53.3 from 56.6 in February.

Interestingly, roughly 2/3 of interviews were completed after the start of the U.S. military conflict in Iran. The data also shows that year-ahead personal finance expectations fell 10%, while one-year business condition expectations fell 14%.

On inflation, the survey shows one-year inflation expectations rising to 3.8% from 3.4% earlier in March.

As always, I take the consumer survey data with a good dose of skepticism. Why?

First, just remember the spike in one-year inflation expectations during last year’s tariff selloff, with values as high as 6.5%.

Second, the Fed's latest SEP is showing a softer impact on inflation. Even though the Fed's Summary of Economic Projections is looking at PCE inflation rather than CPI, the projections for 2026 were revised to only 2.7% from 2.4-2.5% in the previous meeting.

The Market Tape

As the conflict in the Middle East continues, the market tape remains more or less the same: energy is leading in terms of returns, both on red days and green days like today (as a side note, I'm writing this piece in the premarket session of March 30).

Looking at the market breadth in the last month, pretty much every industry within energy has been up in terms of returns, with the exception of uranium.

Notably, the breadth of uranium names in the last month is 0%, meaning that none of the stocks within this industry have shown positive returns, no matter their size. For reference, here is the one-month tile map of uranium stocks.

I am not surprised in the slightest. Why?

In my view, the selloff in uranium stocks is a second-order effect of the selloff in big tech, as a good part of the premium of these stocks is the energy demand from the AI data center buildout.

Even though the returns in tech have been extremely poor in the last month due to the fears of higher rates for longer, the Street's forward expectations for revenue and EPS haven't materially changed.

For reference, here are the Street's revenue expectations for Nvidia for the next four quarters.

Since the war began in the Middle East, the Street's models have remained unchanged, despite the mid-single-digit decline in NVDA shares.

Looking Ahead

Trying to predict President Trump's next move in the war (and trade based on that) makes little to no sense.

Even for someone with a basic understanding of geopolitical conflicts, like myself, it’s quite clear that the surprise factor in invading Kharg Island is now gone. Therefore, I think the chance of such a military operation is slim, as the U.S. troops would become a target as soon as they set foot on the island.

That said, I only see signs of escalation, especially after the Houthi attacks during the weekend.

In the meantime, the Strait of Hormuz remains largely closed, although Trump signaled that Iran had agreed to release 20 more cargo ships of oil through the Strait of Hormuz starting this Monday and continuing over the next few days.

Overall, I prefer to remain on the sidelines, raise even more cash, and not chase the highs in energy stocks. Overall, I haven't seen any capitulation yet in the S&P 500, and I reiterate my price target of $610-$620 in H1 2026 in the SPY.

data.title: Fiverr: Likely Value Trap In The Age Of AI
data.description:
The last time I reviewed Fiverr International Ltd. (FVRR) was in late 2024. At the time I liked that this founder-led company seemed to be leaning into AI, and I thought it might be a beneficiary of a growing gig economy, which is why I gave the company a “Buy” rating.

That’s probably been one (if not my worst) call, as the stock is down more than 60% since the article was published. In 2026 alone, FVRR is down over 50%, while the S&P 500 is down just 7%.

With the increasing capabilities of artificial intelligence, especially the new features of Claude (like Cowork and Code), there’s a huge expectation these tools are going to wipe out a lot of freelance work.

Whether that happens, only time will tell. Let’s dive into the company's plan to combat AI and discuss the company’s business metrics as well as recent financial results.

Will Fiverr's 4 Pillar Plan Be Enough to Combat AI?

In the back half of 2025, Fiverr’s management team announced a multi-year company-wide restructuring plan focused on four key pillars. On the company’s recent earnings call, co-founder and CEO Micha Kaufman outlined this four-pillar plan:

A few months ago, we initiated a company-wide restructuring to accelerate this shift. We have since developed a multiyear execution plan built around 4 pillars: the first is matching, building advanced semantic and reasoning layer powered by proprietary data to enable AI-native talent matching. The second is product, transforming the experience across matching, fulfillment, collaboration and talent management. The third is go-to-market, expanding into enterprise and AI-native distribution channels with scalable growth engines. The fourth is operational excellence, becoming an AI-native organization across engineering, product and operations.

Kaufman went on to say he’s expecting tangible results from this plan in 4 to 6 quarters and believes it will lead to continued revenue expansion.

I’d say the company essentially had to make a strategic move like this one because the business has to move upmarket because AI is eating the small, simple tasks. It’s interesting the company actually noted this risk in their prior year 20-F filing, stating:

AI may present challenges for our industry. There is a risk that the evolving AI landscape could reduce the demand for some of our simple service offerings within the writing, translation, and graphic and design verticals.

In hindsight, “may present challenges” is a massive understatement, as I’d say it’s the primary challenge for the business now. The business is hoping Fiverr Pro can continue to drive upmarket adoption through more premium offerings like the following:

There are some positives for Fiverr, as numerous metrics illustrate the company still has a solid take rate and is expanding wallet share:

It’ll be interesting to see if these numbers continue to hold up as tools like Claude and Gemini continue to improve. Fiverr certainly must continue to expand their AI-native offerings. The company did recently launch an AI video hub. On the whole, it seems like a good move. Fiverr must continue thinking outside the box and create similar offerings going forward.

Fiverr still sees a large market opportunity, which they noted in their last Investor Presentation:

I have to say this TAM assumption seems very bold to me given the situation Fiverr is facing. The core issue is that a good portion of the traditional freelance market, like writing, graphic design, translation, and entry-level coding, is precisely what AI tools are disrupting. If AI continues to commoditize these lower-end services, the effective addressable market for a platform like Fiverr could be materially smaller than the figure above suggests.

It does seem like the gig economy is growing, and many think this trend will continue. I’d venture to guess it does as AI layoffs in industries like technology increase. Yet, an increase in freelancers doesn't automatically translate into growth on Fiverr's platform, especially if buyers turn to AI tools directly.

Let’s next discuss business challenges, as there are more risks than simply just AI.

Risks

Fiverr lists numerous items that might impact the business going forward. I’m going to touch on a few risks noted in the company’s Risk Factors section in their most recent 20-F filing.

Competition remains a top risk. I’d put that into two buckets. You have competition from AI tools, and you have competition from other companies employing freelancers, like Upwork (UPWK). While Fiverr is a known name in the space, compared to Upwork, it’s historically focused on what I’d say are “simple” projects, thus management’s big push to get the business upmarket.

Over the past few years, the business has been able to focus more on high-quality buyers as total buyers have gone down, yet annual spend per buyer is improving, as you can see below:

Fiverr’s challenge will be keeping buyers using the platform. Fiverr noted in the company’s shareholder letter they don’t think these key performance indicators are vital given the new plan. However, I’d say these metrics are important because Fiverr can’t keep relying on higher-value customers. I’d say they need to try to add users or at least stop the bleeding, yet I’m not sure if that’s possible given the AI alternatives.

Fiverr does have cash on hand as their balance sheet remains in solid shape (which I’ll discuss more below). Management has stated capital will likely go to M&A. This could certainly enhance Fiverr’s offerings and perhaps even help the business acquire new buyers. Yet it could lead to Peter Lynch’s infamous “diworsification.” Given the current state of affairs, any unsuccessful M&A activity would certainly hurt the company's financial position (for example, wasting cash or having goodwill impairment).

Finally, from a risk perspective, I’d say Fiverr could be impacted by worsening macro conditions. While layoffs could lead to more gig workers, as many of Fiverr’s customers are SMBs, worsening conditions like inflation or a recession would certainly affect Fiverr from both a seller and buyer perspective.

Financials

Before diving into the current 20-F filing, let’s zoom out and see how well Fiverr has performed on an annual basis over the last few years. As you can see from the below graphic from the company’s latest Investor Presentation, the company has seen revenue growth continue over the last few years:

Furthermore, despite increases in investments (for example, the company’s AI capabilities), Fiverr’s net income and earnings per share have continuously improved, as you can see below:

Additionally, thanks to share buybacks, the company’s share count has also declined, which is a positive for investors. Management noted that as of December 31, 2025, the company still has $67.5 million left on the current authorization for the buyback program. Although, I’d like to see the company use their cash to focus on internal investments or M&A.

These trends are positive, but things change when you look at upcoming guidance. For fiscal year 2026, management is expecting revenue in the range of $380 million to $420 million, which would be a decline in the range of 3% to 12%. Adjusted EBITDA is expected to be in the range of $60 to $80 million. That’s quite a drop from the $92 million that company earned in fiscal year 2025.

Fiverr still has a solid balance sheet, as you can see below, with enough current assets to cover all of their current liabilities and no long-term debt:

Valuation

Fiverr has a valuation grade of an “A+” at Seeking Alpha:

Whichever way you choose to value this company, all these metrics would lead an investor to think Fiverr is trading at a very cheap valuation. As it’s a profitable, more established company, I’d say price to earnings is the best valuation metric for this business.

If you look at the company's forward P/E ratio (Non-GAAP), it's lower than the sector median and significantly lower than the company's historic five-year averages. In the below graphic, you can see Fiverr's current forward P/E ratio is at an all-time low:

From a different viewpoint, I have updated my reverse discounted cash flow model using the latest cash flow figures for Fiverr. Using a discount rate of 10% and a terminal rate of 2%, if you look at analysts' revenue growth estimates, most believe Fiverr's revenue will be flat or grow in the low single digits on average for the next several years. If Fiverr can grow even by a meager 1%, it would be viewed as undervalued, as you can see below:

Conclusion

Historically, Fiverr has proven over the last few years that despite losing buyers, they have been able to get their current buyers to spend more and thus have continued to generate revenue growth.

2026 is shaping up to be a much different story. On one hand, I think there has been an exaggeration as to how quickly AI implementation will impact businesses, especially SMBs. Yet, Claude’s new capabilities are impressive, and given the fact Google and Microsoft are spending immensely on capex, AI tools and enhancements will only make life easier for many buyers who would have traditionally used freelancers.

As Kaufman noted on the earnings call, “There's a prevailing narrative that AI eliminates labor. That framing is incomplete.”

While incomplete, I’d say it will eliminate at least some labor, and despite a growing gig economy, I’m not sure if Fiverr will be able to compete with such intense competition.

While the stock is probably oversold at the minute, I’d only look at this as a trading, bounce-back play. The long-term narrative is too uncertain despite the cheap valuation. I'll be watching and waiting to see how Kaufman’s new strategy plays out in 2026.

data.title: NetApp: Rising Billings Against A Low P/E
data.description:
Very few investors will disagree that 2026 has been an incredibly challenging environment to invest in. After the recent war-driven selloff, few investors are willing to brave the choppy markets, especially to invest in tech stocks that have been battered by the so-called “SaaSpocalypse.” But for investors whose time horizons are long, I continue to encourage active portfolio management and to take advantage of dislocated valuations as a buying opportunity.

NetApp (NTAP), in particular, is an increasingly attractive bargain. The managed storage solutions provider has seen its share price decline ~5% since the start of the year, despite already starting off as a bargain stock. Meanwhile, underlying performance appears to be gaining ground, making now a potentially attractive buying window in this stock.

I last wrote a "Buy" article on NetApp in November, when the stock was trading at $112 per share. With NetApp’s decline in share prices since then, I continue to see a buying opportunity here in a stock that is seeing firming execution and has little risk of being displaced by AI. I’m reiterating my "Buy" rating here.

For investors who are newer to NetApp, here are what I find to be the core bull case drivers for the company:

  • Secular tailwinds as AI prompts businesses to reevaluate their infrastructure stacks. NetApp is universally applicable to all industries; the company’s storage and data management products are relevant to companies in all verticals. As companies review their IT stacks amid the push to deploy AI applications, NetApp will benefit from increased storage needs and more complex workloads.
  • Pass-through of memory price increases. As many investors are aware, memory/NAND chips are in severely constrained supply. NetApp has passed on price increases to its customers without any seeming impact on demand, while its gross margins have remained high and intact in the low 70s.
  • Hybrid data management. NetApp allows for full customer flexibility, whether they want to deploy storage management in a public cloud, an on-prem environment, or a hybrid mix. It also offers flash and non-flash storage options, which differentiates it from all-flash rivals, offering a key affordability driver amid rising flash memory prices.
  • Close to Rule of 40 operating metrics. The company balances a mid-single-digit revenue growth rate alongside low 30s pro forma operating margins, putting NetApp just on the cusp of the Rule of 40.

However, it’s valuation that remains the defining reason to invest in NetApp. For FY26 (the year for NetApp ending in April 2026), the company has guided to $6.772-$6.922 billion in revenue, or 2%-5% growth, and $7.92-$8.02 in pro forma EPS (+10% midpoint EPS growth).

And for FY27, Street estimates call for $7.16 billion in revenue (+5% y/y) and $8.51 in EPS (+7% y/y). This positions NetApp’s valuation multiples at:

  • 12.8x FY26 P/E
  • 12.0x FY27 P/E

Even in a battered stock market, NetApp’s valuation discounts stand out starkly. NetApp’s multiples more closely resemble an industrial company that is facing transitional challenges, when its true reality is just the opposite: the company is benefiting from AI workloads and the related investments into infrastructure.

Stay long here and use the dip as a buying opportunity.

Q3 download

Let’s now go through NetApp’s latest fiscal Q3 (January quarter) results in greater detail. The Q3 earnings summary is shown below:

NetApp’s revenue grew 4% y/y to $1.71 billion, beating Wall Street expectations of $1.70 billion (+3% y/y) by a one-point margin. Revenue growth also accelerated slightly versus 3% growth in Q2.

Where we are most impressed in NetApp’s quarter, however, is in billings. As software investors are aware, billings is one of the best forward-looking indicators for a recurring revenue software company, as it captures deals signed in the quarter that will be recognized as revenue in future quarters. In Q3, NetApp’s billings rebounded to 10% growth, up six points sequentially from 4% growth in Q2.

NetApp is up against a fairly challenging environment. IT budgets are constrained, and memory price increases (which NetApp has passed onto customers) further complicate that equation. And yet NetApp’s billings acceleration is a strong signal that AI-related infrastructure investments are still driving net new business, while NetApp’s wide array of flash and non-flash storage options appeal to customers of varying needs.

Per CEO George Kurian’s remarks on the Q3 earnings call:

Before I wrap up, I'd like to address how we are managing through the unprecedented inflation in memory prices currently affecting the global market. First, we have raised our pricing and will do so again, as needed. Second, we are working with our customers and channel partners to be more agile in this dynamic environment. Third, we are working with our multiple suppliers to address availability and manage costs, as we have successfully done in the past. And finally, unlike our all-flash only competitors, we have a broad portfolio that includes hybrid flash arrays, giving us the opportunity to better service price-sensitive workloads.

In summary, solid execution and operational discipline delivered another strong quarter. Customers are choosing NetApp for our unified data platform that delivers exceptional value and operational efficiencies, solidifying our position as the intelligent data backbone for the AI era.

Meanwhile, the company’s all-flash array annualized revenue run rate also jumped at a healthy 11% y/y pace to $4.2 billion:

NetApp also continued to make consistent progress toward profitability. Pro forma gross margins rose 50bps y/y to 71.2%, reflecting growing economies of scale and NetApp’s successful price increases.

Meanwhile, pro forma operating expenses also only rose 2.5% y/y to 40.0% of revenue, down slightly from 40.8% in the year-ago quarter. As a result, pro forma operating margins rose 110bps y/y to 31.1%:

NetApp’s pro forma EPS of $2.12 also rose 11% y/y and beat Wall Street’s expectations of $2.06 with 3% upside.

Key takeaways

In my view, there's a lot to like about NetApp in 2026. The company is delivering improved billings performance, which is a clear signal that AI workloads are beginning to more meaningfully impact NetApp's deal flow. The company continues to improve its gross and operating margin profile, which is feeding double-digit EPS growth that makes the stock's low-teens P/E multiples look modest. Stay long here.

data.title: Q2 2026 U.S. Indices (Dow Jones, S&P 500 & Nasdaq 100) Outlook - Resilience Or Retracement?
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

As we pull the curtain on a turbulent first quarter, the narrative for US equities in Q2 2026 is shifting from "all-out AI" to a more nuanced, "pensive" reality. The bullish momentum that defined 2025 has hit a significant roadblock, primarily driven by the escalation of conflict in the Middle East and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz.

For market participants, the question isn’t just whether the trend is still your friend, but whether you have the stomach for the "TACO" trade (Trump Always Comes Off), the market's growing belief that the administration will pivot to prevent a total equity meltdown.

The Macro Backdrop: Oil shocks and a Fed cornered

The primary headwind for Q2 is undeniably the energy market. With Brent crude spiking above $100/barrel, headline inflation is rearing its head again. S&P Global warns that while the US is better insulated as a net exporter than in decades past, the "inflationary consequences" are unavoidable.

This leaves the Fed in a precarious position. Markets are now pricing in a "higher-for-longer" scenario as the central bank balances supply-driven inflation against a cooling labor market. While a 25-bps cut is still penciled in for late 2026, the Q2 outlook remains a "growth scare" rather than an imminent recession.

The legacy of "Liberation Day" and the 2025 tariff regime

To understand the price action of Q2 2026, one must think of it against the shift in trade policy that occurred on April 2, 2025, known as "Liberation Day."

This executive order introduced a sweeping 10% reciprocal tariff on all imports, with significantly higher rates for countries like China (up to 45% when combined with previous measures), Canada, and Mexico. The immediate reaction in 2025 was a historic "shock to the system," causing the S&P 500 to flirt with bear market territory and the Nasdaq to enter one briefly in April 2025.

However, the "Liberation Day" theme in 2026 has changed from a source of panic into a valuation test.

Many of the leading technology firms, particularly the "Magnificent Seven," are currently trading at valuation levels that are nearly as attractive as the troughs reached during the initial 2025 tariff sell-off. The lessons of 2025 taught the market that while tariffs introduce policy uncertainty and alter expected returns, the resilience of the US economy, boosted by tax rebates and a pivot toward domestic manufacturing, can eventually stabilize sentiment.

As we move through Q2 2026, the question is whether the market can repeat this stabilization in the face of a secondary geopolitical shock.

US Indices: Technical Levels and the Search for a Floor

The S&P 500 cash index found major resistance at the 7,000 level in late January 2026, a psychological and technical barrier that triggered a significant reversal. Since then, the index has been searching for a durable support zone.

  • Primary Support at 6145: This support zone is directly linked to the price action seen post-Liberation Day in 2025. It serves as a historical "floor" where institutional buyers have previously stepped in.
  • The 6000 Level: This level may come into play and represents a crucial area for bulls to defend. A failure here would open the door to a deeper correction.
  • Outlook: The current sentiment is one of "opportunistic bullishness." Rather than chasing the bearish momentum, market participants should look for signs of a turn higher near these key support levels, supported by the expectation of a market-friendly Federal Reserve.

Something that piqued my interest this week was historical performance based on the first 59 days of the year. The table below outlines the 20 worst starts to a year for the S&P 500 (based on the first 59 trading days) from 1928 through 2026.

The most striking observation is that a bad start does not guarantee a bad year. In fact, the market often staged massive recoveries.

Positive Finishes: Out of the 19 completed years on this "Worst Starts" list, 11 of them (58%) ended the full calendar year in positive territory.

The "V" Recovery: In many cases, the "Day 60 to Year-End" returns were not just positive, but explosive.

For example:

1933: Fell -12.6% early, then rallied +64.8% to finish up +44.1%.

2020: Fell -18.6% (the worst start on record), then rallied +42.8% to finish up +16.3%.

The Nasdaq 100 (NDX): AI Exhaustion vs. Structural Growth

The Nasdaq 100 is currently grappling with "AI exhaustion," as the initial frenzy surrounding artificial intelligence has given way to a more sober assessment of valuation and capital expenditure. The weekly chart has become difficult to justify as bullish in the short term, as price action breaks through established support levels.

  • Major Support at 22500: This zone represented major resistance in 2025 and is now the first line of defense for the tech sector.
  • The 20000 Psychological Handle: A major zone spanning from 20,000 to 20,224, which aligns with a 61.8% Fibonacci retracement of the recent multi-year move. This is arguably the most critical support level for the NDX in 2026.
  • Fundamental Counterpoint: Despite technical weakness, the "AI data-center build" remains in its early stages. Top technology firms are on track to spend $700 billion on capex in 2026, a 36% year-over-year increase. This acyclic investment provides a fundamental "floor" for the index that traditional technical analysis might overlook.

Nasdaq 100 Daily Chart, March 30, 2026

The Dow Jones Industrial Average: A Potential Value Haven

The Dow Jones Industrial Average is viewed as offering a potentially more attractive backdrop for bullish continuation compared to the tech-heavy indices. Having stalled at the 50,000 handle, the Dow is testing support levels that could offer a more stable entry point.

  • Key Support at 45244: This level represents a 100% measured move of the 2022 sell-off and is a major technical pivot.
  • Secondary Support at 43,325: A prior resistance-turned-support zone that has historical significance.
  • Outlook: If buyers can stage a defense near the 45,000 psychological level in early Q2, the Dow may lead a broader market recovery as investors rotate out of overvalued growth and into resilient industrial and financial names.

Dow Jones Daily Chart, March 30, 2026

Original Post

data.title: BNO: A Primer On The Only Pure Play Brent Crude ETF
data.description:
Introduction To The United States Brent Oil Fund

The United States Brent Oil Fund LP ETF (BNO), which was set up as a Delaware limited partnership on September 2, 2009, by United States Commodity Funds, LLC (USCF) (a Californian-based provider of commodity-based ETFs) but only began trading on the markets on June 1, 2010, has, so far, managed to garner total assets under management (AUM) of over $830M. BNO has an expense ratio of 1.14% and doesn't pay any distributions to its holders.

What Does BNO Do?

BNO may be an ETF, but it is structured as a Limited Partnership (LP), which means when investors buy shares of BNO, they are essentially buying shares of a partnership whereby USCF (the issuer) serves as the general partner. So, what does this partnership entail?

Well, BNO’s goal is to serve as an accessible vehicle for market players to play the daily price moments of Brent crude oil (rather than the price changes of the lighter, sweeter, US-centric alternative of WTI crude), and the former attempts to do so by investing in crude oil future contracts (more commonly known as futures). To get more specific, at the start of every month, BNO holds the near-month futures contract of Brent until the last two weeks of the expiration of said contract (after which it will gradually shift to the next month's contract of Brent futures during a 4-day period).

For those not familiar with futures contracts, these are standardized and highly liquid financial contracts traded on a certain exchange (in this case, the Intercontinental Exchange, also known as ICE) that enable their holders to be exposed to the movements of certain commodity prices without necessarily owning those commodities physically. Investors could perhaps imagine how buying, transporting, holding, and selling physical barrels of oil in gargantuan quantities would come across as very impractical and cumbersome for the standard individual investor. Against that overbearing scenario, one could view these oil futures contracts as a panacea of sorts that helps negate those concerns while still serving as a metaphorical thermometer of the demand-supply conditions that affect the Brent crude landscape.

Now, while the bulk of BNO’s portfolio consists of Brent futures, it also maintains some collateral in the form of liquid securities (US money market and treasury securities) and cash to back up those future contracts. Thus, the returns that BNO could provide won’t just be limited to the movements of Brent crude futures but also the income generated from the liquid securities. Before those returns can be passed on to investors, don’t also forget that various expenses related to the management of BNO also need to be met.

Who Is BNO For?

BNO’s focus on Brent crude makes it ideal for those who have a clear perspective on the supply/demand drivers of international oil rather than US-based oil.

Given BNO’s focus on near-month futures contracts, it’s fair to say that it is better suited for oil market players who want to take a short-term, tactical perspective on Brent crude rather than those who are looking to make a long-term call, akin to the motivations of a buy-and-hold-type investor.

BNO’s suitability as a short-term vehicle can also be gleaned from the impressive degree of daily dollar volumes that it experiences on a daily basis. To elaborate, while most ETFs only witness daily dollar volumes amounting to less than $2M per day, BNO sees volumes that are over 100x greater! For added context, also consider that while most ETFs only see around 1-2% of their total AUM get turned over every day, BNO’s daily dollar volumes of $165M per day, implies that a sizeable 20% of its total AUM gets turned over on a daily basis! These strong daily dollar volumes also mean that the difference between BNO’s bid and ask is very tight at just $0.01, thus pretty much insulating investors from the risk of slippages.

Since we’ve highlighted BNO’s superior liquidity qualities, what sort of an environment would be best suited for this product?

When spot oil prices are on the rise and when the oil market is dealing with a backwardation backdrop. Backwardation in the oil markets is marked by high current demand and grave supply shortages, so much so that the price of near-month futures contracts is higher than the price for future-month contracts. Graphically, if one were to plot the different futures contracts by tenure in a backwardation backdrop (as is the case as of March 2026), we would have a downward-sloping curve like what you see below.

Since we know that BNO rolls its contracts every month, in an environment of backwardation, it is essentially selling high-priced near-month contracts and then, during the roll transition, is buying relatively cheaper next-month contracts. That difference between the two contracts (the roll yield) gives another boost to BNO’s total returns besides the standard rise in spot prices.

Given that oil and its derivative products are used in an overwhelming range of industries as raw materials, those who pursue BNO may also be looking to hedge against the rising prices of goods and services or the loss of purchasing power.

What Are The Risks Associated With BNO?

Like most commodity products, BNO is very susceptible to geopolitical risks and the appreciation of the US Dollar.

As is the case with most single-commodity products, the risk profile of this product is on the higher side of the ETF spectrum. To get more specific, while most ETFs experience annualized volatility of only around 17% on average, BNO’s volatility profile is well over twice as high and suggests that it won’t be suitable for those investors who expect to see some stability in their portfolio’s readings.

While backwardation is good for BNO, the opposite can be said for an environment marked by contango. Here, when futures prices are higher than current prices, BNO is faced with the scenario of selling low and buying high, and when this adds up every month, this can cause a major dent to overall returns, even if spot Brent prices stay relatively flat.

For an ETF that is seen as a short-term vehicle, its expense ratio of 1.14% (which is higher than other alternatives that focus on WTI crude) is not too pleasing. Moreover, investors don’t receive any compensatory distributions to ameliorate the high-cost burden.

Taxation and administration practices associated with BNO are not particularly straightforward and could prove to be a hassle for a fair few investors. Since this product is structured as an LP, investors won’t receive a 1099-B form highlighting the internal activity that has been witnessed over a year; rather, what you get is a schedule K-1, which has also been notorious for arriving at irregular time frames, prompting tax filing delays.

Since BNO is not structured as a regular ETF but as an LP that deals with commodity futures, its holdings at the end of every year need to be marked to market (ascertaining the difference between the fair value of these contracts from their cost basis). Thus, even if investors of BNO have not sold their holdings, but these contracts have gained in price, they will have to pay taxes on their pro-rata share of the funds gains. Also, the futures contracts that BNO holds are typically qualified as “section 1256 contracts,” which makes them subject to the 60/40 tax rule. Here, regardless of how long an investor has owned BNO, 60% of any gains will be taxed at the long-term capital gains rate, with only 40% being taxed at the investor’s tax bracket rate.

What Are The Alternatives To BNO?

BNO may be viewed as something of a specialized case, as it is currently the only US-listed ETF that offers pure-play coverage to “Brent oil” futures. However, if prospective investors are not overly fixated on this international oil benchmark and don’t mind pursuing products that are rather oriented around the US oil benchmark, “WTI crude,” then there are quite a few cheaper (in terms of the expense ratio) and older alternatives that could be pursued, such as

  • United States Oil Fund, LP (USO).
  • United States 12 Month Oil Fund, LP (USL).
  • Invesco DB Oil Fund (DBO).

In terms of structure, USO, the most popular product of this lot (AUM of $2.65B, with even higher daily volumes), is the closest peer of BNO. Like BNO, it too favors the near-month futures strategy, but the focus here is on WTI crude contracts. USO, which made it to the bourses (in April 2006) before all these alternatives, also stands out due to its inordinately high level of daily liquidity (which is even larger than its AUM) and speaks to how well it is seen as a spot price proxy. The focus on near-month contracts also makes it vulnerable to environments of contango.

USL and DBO are different from BNO and USO in that they are not overly focused on the near-month futures strategy. DBO, which is the odd one out in terms of the issuer (Invesco Capital Management) and is also the most cost-efficient of the lot, is best suited for those who want to ameliorate the risk of contango. We say this because DBO basically tracks a rule-based optimized index (DBIQ Optimum Yield Crude Oil Index Excess Return) that scans the futures curve over the next 13 months and picks the contract that will lose the least in eras of contango. Likewise, during phases of backwardation, it focuses on contracts that will have the highest roll yield.

USL, the least popular product (AUM of only $62M and daily dollar volumes of around $1M) from this peer set, stands out from the rest because it employs a balanced and laddered strategy, whereby rather than rolling its portfolio every month (like BNO and USO) or choosing to pursue the futures contract with the most optimum yield, it buys equal amounts of futures contracts with differing durations over the next 12 months (so near-month WTI contract exposure is just a little over 8%, i.e., 1/12). This makes it more suitable as a long-term vehicle than the others that are more attuned to short-term movements. Also, since BNO is spreading its wings across contracts for a full year, the roll risk is diminished.

Summary

BNO, which is structured as a limited partnership, offers investors a route to play the Brent crude market through near-month futures contracts. While there are other alternatives that dabble with WTI crude futures, BNO is the only US-listed ETF that covers Brent crude futures.

This article answers these three main questions about BNO:

  • What does BNO do?
  • What type of investor is BNO suitable for, and what are the risks associated with it?
  • What are the alternatives to BNO?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: Micron: The Competition Is Catching Up
data.description:
Introduction

Micron (MU) has been seeing its stock decline from its peaks despite the company reporting record-breaking guidance for its Q2 earnings that would, in essence, triple the company’s financials compared to 2 years ago. I’ll dive into why I still believe that Micron is not worth an investment at this current price, as it is down more than 4% since my sell rating on the company.

Current Dynamics

The main driver of the decline has been Micron’s decision to increase its fiscal 2026 Capex guidance by over $25B. Though this is to fast-track domestic manufacturing, the hike gave the signal of the ever-coming oversupply trap if the AI boom slows even at a slim margin.

More recently, the ratepayer protection pledge has forced the hyperscalers like Amazon (AMZN) and Google (GOOG) to internalize long-dated energy utility liabilities. There are some concerns that these infrastructure premiums will directly put costs onto tech balance sheets and potentially cool the available capital for HBM. This ties in with the $100B New York megafab that has faced significant timeline resets, as the first fab will now start in Q3 of 2030 due to labor shortages and construction complexities. This shows the difference between theoretical planning and the real-world friction I mentioned in my earlier analysis, which would certainly affect such a large-scale endeavor.

Another pillar of the bull case behind Micron has been Agentic AI, which can autonomously plan, reason, and act to do multi-step tasks. The nature of agentic AI, which can generate thousands of continuous inference loops per user session, in essence acts as a sustained baseload of compute demand, multiplying the amount of HBM required per user by an order of magnitude. However, that monetization wall has remained a risk, as while reports have indicated that 79% of organizations have adopted AI agents, only 34% have achieved full implementation. More so, research from S&P Global suggests that 80% to 95% of enterprise AI pilots fail to deliver expected returns. If hyperscalers like Microsoft (MSFT) or Google believe that they have overbuilt their data centers before these agentic systems produce clear corporate profits, the demand for $10,000 HBM modules could evaporate overnight.

Though Micron has captured the highest-margin slice of the HBM4 market, competitors have crept in, such as Samsung (SSNLF) and SK hynix, which are prone to take back their lost market share. Samsung, after struggling for a couple of years, has now regained the top spot in the DRAM market as of the start of the year. Samsung effectively passed Nvidia’s HBM4 qualification tests with data rates exceeding 10 Gb/s and 11/Gb/s, and began volume shipments in February 2026. On the side of SK hynix, it still remains the dominant supplier of HBM despite losing some of its market share to Micron. The overall risk for Micron is that as these two ramp up their massive production capacity and start a price war to reclaim market share, forcing the highly touted margins to erode through 2026 and 2027, which I believe the market has not yet priced in.

It is important to note that Micron is undergoing one of its fastest tech ramp-ups to keep its competitive edge. MU is pushing the 1-gamma DRAM, which is expected to become the highest-volume node in company history, with ramping volumes faster than all previous ones. Likewise, the G9 node is scheduled to be the majority of NAND bits by mid-2026, which is set to drive record revenue in the storage business unit.

Valuation

I get the argument that Micron is undeniably cheap considering the growth of its bottom line and the current trajectory of the firm’s business. It currently has a P/E GAAP of 16.72, drastically lower than Samsung or Broadcom (AVGO) (SK hynix was not included due to the lack of data on SA). On an FCF view, the company’s appreciation has turned its relatively high historical yield into a mere 2.4% at current prices. I also believe that it is important to go beyond just valuation metrics, and that’s why I made a scenario and cycle analysis. Based on the blend of scenarios, with a higher weight towards bull and base, the target comes out to $254 per share, a downside of 28.1%.

By looking at the past cycles of margin contraction, if Samsung and SK hynix were to compete for market share and fight price-wise, the implied trough could be much harsher, considering the height to which Micron’s revenue has reached. The implied stock price decline would push the stock towards the $181 range per share.

On the growth prospects front, the main argument that can fight off the competition would be the increase in the overall TAM for HBM, which is now projected to reach $100B by 2028, a milestone pulled forward by 2 years due to the insatiable AI demand. Micron's HBM4 is already shipping in volume and is designed for Nvidia’s Vera Rubin architecture. The data center segment is expected to exceed 50% of the total industry memory TAM thanks to the AI accelerators and a robust refresh of traditional servers.

Risks

The biggest risk to my thesis would be the fact that Micron has started to shift and sign 5-year SCAs with major customers, a massive departure from the traditional 1-year agreement. These contracts include specific multi-year volume and pricing commitments, providing unprecedented visibility and stability that did not exist in previous cycles.

Though the exit of the Crucial brand removes a consumer avenue, especially in times of excess inventory, MU now creates a voluntary shortage that maintains high pricing across all segments due to the extreme wafer intensity of HBM. Analysts also believe that the demand is so strong that Micron does not need a low-margin consumer outlet to manage capacity.

Conclusion

Yes, Micron is doing all it can to remain at the forefront of the AI industry’s needs, but cracks are beginning to form, especially as we look at the New York megafab or the monetization challenges of Agentic AI. The revenue growth potential is undeniably impressive, but the LT expansion plan is being built on a foundation of unproven AI productivity gains and a highly concentrated customer demand while peers catch up. I keep my sell rating.
